speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three of the interview with Dr. Fritz Gluckstein. You were describing the events of Kristallnacht. Would you please continue? Could you tell us about what happened to the synagogues where your family-- where you and your father had worship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most synagogues, actually, were burned. To the best of my knowledge, I've heard that the police and fire department were told to stay away. But the synagogue, the one I became bar mitzvahed on, the following thing happened. I think the hoodlums came in there and started a fire. And the fire department waited outside. They set the fire and left and immediately came in. And what was burned were two rows of benches. And therefore, certain fire departments did their very best. And some other synagogues were saved too. But the-- the majority just burned down. And the fire department was looking out that nothing happened to the neighboring houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were on the site, but they weren't intervening unless neighbors--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some-- but some intervened. The one in the synagogue probably did not argue with those gangs, just let them do it. Then when they left, they just rushed in and-- oh, that was it. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What became of the congregations? Where did they continue to worship if their synagogues were destroyed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, what they did, they went to the synagogues that were still standing. Some only partially burned. They refurbished it to a certain degree. But you must remember, starting pretty soon after that, people voluntarily or involuntarily left. You see there fewer and fewer people.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- they voluntarily left--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some could still immigrate. And others then, in 1940, the so-called immigration started therefore. But some synagogues were still used.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do-- do you recall that night? Do you remember sirens or sounds?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I do not. I-- when I came out in the morning, I was surprised seeing the broken glass all over. Then it occurred to me what had happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was-- what was the response of the populace of Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Populace. I saw nobody applauding. Of course, you must realize perhaps it was not because they were philo-Semitic but because probably some, at least, older. Older is very important. But I must say, no, there were-- I didn't see. Understand, some neighborhoods really applauded. But not where I lived. I did not see that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if-- if Berlin-- if the Berlin Jewish community was held financially responsible for restoring the streets and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah. In some way they actually were quite responsible. Their actions, or the actions of that assassin Grynszpan, were responsible. They afterward claimed, not special gangs, but the population reacted. And they did. And just because the Jews provoked the population, they caused, actually, the damage themselves and had to pay for the cleanup. And also they were assessed quite a tremendous fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And individuals paid that fine? Or did the Jewish gemeinde pay?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish gemeinde, we had to pay it in some way. I'm not quite clear how it happened. But I know they had to pay. I still remember the taxes. Actually, they were put into higher tax brackets anyway, the Jews were, I recall that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what happened after Kristallnacht? Did-- did things escalate? Did the situation get worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, yes usually. Then no cars, and-- I guess I mentioned it before-- no physicians and dentists for-- only Jewish physicians and Jewish dentists were permitted only to take Jews. Actually, then, actually, next milestone was outbreak of the war. In '38 the Kristallnacht, and realize it was in November '38. And then it's August '39, the war broke out. And then things really started to get going.

speaker: interviewer
text: What impact did the beginning of the war have on-- on the Jewish community in Berlin? And in-- on Berlin community in general?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, number one, the-- the ration cards started out. Number one, see a special-- already special ration cards for Jews. But it did in the beginning, the ration cards had coupons. And what happened, the Jews were only permitted to shop between 4:00 and 5:00 in the afternoon. But what at times happened, you just gave some good friend, you gave your coupons and he could buy it. Well, later on, each coupon had the J or Jew on it. And that couldn't be done anymore. And immediately, no phones, no radio. And you had to deliver furs, and field glasses, and jewelry. And actually what they did, they pulled students out of high school, at least, to help in the collection of those furs. I remember what we did, it was actually one of the synagogue had to deliver at synagogue. And we actually collected it.

speaker: interviewer
text: They used Jewish students from the schools to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were these collections usually taken care of by the Jewish community, by the gemeinde?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they used a community of-- yes. They were told to deliver all your furs and jewelry. Probably they estimated how much they expected. And if not, probably I don't know what they would have done. But we collected the fur. I remember distinctly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of people holding back? Holding on to radios? Or refusing to relinquish items?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to my knowledge. It was a radio, in fact. I don't think a Jew would have kept a radio. Don't forget, anybody-- it was a danger that the neighbor overheard it and would have reported you. No. I don't know what happened. Apparently some people probably, in some way, gave their jewelry to non-Jewish friends. I think that probably has happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what the consequences would have been for not complying with these regulations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I can tell you. They would have wound up in the headquarters of the Gestapo, Albrecht Street. Not to be confused with Eichmann, but that was the department of the Gestapo that dealt with, well, spies and people who broke the rules and so on. It was very simple. And of course, later on, once it started out with the deportation immediately.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm curious. How did they, the authorities, make known new regulations or summonses to appear for deportations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some were actually-- I heard about it. Apparently my parents read about it in the Jewish newspapers or probably even fliers. There are others, however, certain regulations which are only by mouth. For instance, we weren't permitted to be out after 8 o'clock at night. Now that was actually-- that was transmitted by mouth.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember some friends, for some reason, had to stay out after 8 o'clock. Went to the police precinct for permission to stay out. And said, what are you talking about? We don't know anything about it. But this was actually Gestapo rule.

speaker: interviewee
text: Furthermore, you were not permitted to-- certain streets were off limits. Those were informal. The police wouldn't know about it. But we didn't go. We were not permitted to be in certain streets. Otherwise, the regulation were made by newspaper. See, I only knew I was told, no, starting today, this or that. And starting tomorrow no [? babbas ?]. You can't use the [? babbas ?]. And our pets, you have to get rid of the dog, you see. I can only-- I was told. Where it actually appeared I don't know. I would assume the papers. Obviously, I didn't read the papers every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now were the schools still operating?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schools were still operating. We were pulled off the special details. In the summertime, we worked at the cemetery, the Jewish cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they didn't have enough caretakers. So we helped to clean up the-- the path between the graves overgrown with weeds. We helped there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now had the law been passed yet requiring that Jewish students only attend all-Jewish schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: That had already been passed.

speaker: interviewer
text: That had already been passed?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were only Jewish schools. Actually, what happened, at the time when it was passed, there were about four, or five, or six Jewish schools. And I'm talking about high schools, private ones. Some actually were run by the congregation, others private. And-- but bit by bit, people integrated and they combined more and more. And eventually it was only one Jewish high school, particularly after the deportations started. There was one school that-- classes were larger, yes. And, of course, we had some difficulties. You can imagine. There were conservative schools, lots of Hebrew, and the Reformed school, little Hebrew. Now they suddenly got together. And, well, I remember there was at least one [INAUDIBLE] quite a bit of Hebrew. And was six-- at least six parallels. One never actually almost could speak Hebrew, and others that could barely read. And I remember you learned-- they tried to teach us quite a bit of Hebrew. I still, even to the grammar, I still get nightmares what they did. [NON-ENGLISH] People tell me I shouldn't have been that frightened. But for some reason, they frightened us. But it was quite difficult, what to do with the various levels of Hebrew. But I managed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you would have been coming up on your 13th birthday about now, wouldn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is correct. Actually, at that, I still remember distinctly bar mitzvah. At that time was rationing, of course. The-- the synagogue, the one that was saved by the fire department, was still there. And they were scheduled to have the bar mitzvah. Actually, it was so cold at that time, they didn't have it in the main sanctuary. They had a kind of-- I think the-- it was the-- the everyday sanctuary, I think it was. But I remember distinctly. And actually, at that time, my mother went around to certain bakeries. And you could persuade them, they were nice to-- we got some cake and sweets above the ration card. I remember that distinctly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which synagogue did you become bar mitzvah in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Levetzow Street. The same one I mentioned where-- that--

speaker: interviewer
text: And that had very little damage to it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, thanks to the fire department who just waited for the gangs to leave and rushed in immediately. I've been told you could see it. The first two rows were actually immediately put the fire out about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what exactly did you do? What were you responsible for doing as the bar mitzvah boy at the service?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, I believe I actually didn't even read from the Torah. I just said the brachot. And then actually it was all-- before that, they had to have special instruction. But at that time for the Hebrew I read. It wasn't too much time to get the instruction. Therefore, I-- I even knew my [NON-ENGLISH], the laws. But actually I had-- before I actually-- to bar mitzvah, I had to pass an examination. I had to appear before the rabbi. And-- but I still remember it was very nice. And each bar mitzvah got a set of prayer books.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a large-- were there many people present in the synagogue that Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too many. It was, I would say, about 50, 60 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm curious about spiritual life in general. With the imposition of all the regulations, was access to religious worship services curtailed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no that was fine. They did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people continued to go to synagogues?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. At time they don't. But they did afterwards. If they needed people for transport, they were just waiting. When they came out of the synagogue, they grabbed them. That happened too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was actually quite dangerous to go to a synagogue because you might be picked up off the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: You never knew what happened. Well, actually, it was dangerous to run around because of the star. If they needed people, they just grab you on the street. That happened too. Yes, I know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had-- had that regulation come down yet, where Jews already required to wear the Judenstern?

speaker: interviewee
text: This came in 1941. I remember 1940, if I recall correctly, the first evacuations started, primarily in the eastern part of Germany, not so much in Berlin. But then in '41 came the star. I remember rabbi came around from class to class and told us, well, that in some way gave us moral support. Then they had the star. It had to be worn right above the heart, and also a white star on every apartment door too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were people required to move to-- were they required to live in a specific sector of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, what happened very often, for some reason, maybe the neighbors didn't like it. Or somebody wanted their apartment, or for some reason, they were asked to move. And what happened then, they had to move with some other Jewish family. Usually what happened, more and more families lived in one apartment. The time where each family had its own apartment was over. Our first move we had to move, was a move from the West Berlin to the center of Berlin. And there we shared an apartment with another family.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were you in the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were three-- five. We were two and became three. Suddenly more than doubled.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there an influx of Jews from outlying smaller towns, or villages, or rural communities near Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: In retrospect, I would say yes. At that time, I wasn't aware of it. Actually, probably my parents knew about it. But actually, we were either concerned with school and afterwards with the work we had to do. I'm quite sure my parents kept from me as much as they could, see.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did you become aware of deportations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew that friends would get the so-called list at the beginning if someone was selected. How and why, actually, nobody knew. He got the list. And on that list, they had to list their property. And after he filled it out, sent it back to the office, one of the Gestapo. After a while we were notified, you will be deported. [INAUDIBLE] that date. Hold yourself ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then some of-- two-- the Gestapo or sometimes plain-clothed police came and more or less had the list, went through the rooms to make absolutely sure most of the stuff was still there. And then they sealed the apartment. And the person was to report to one of the-- they were [GERMAN]. What would you call it? This kind of transit camp. And they collected people there until there was a large transport.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And when there's some-- today, maybe two days, and then they were sent by trucks to the railroad station, put in railroad cars and left. And of course, my mother frequently helped people wait there till the people came and helped them to carry their luggage to the collection center, the transit camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were people allowed to take with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: What they could carry. In fact, something else. There were some transit camps, they actually pulled us out of class to receive and help the people at the transit camps. See, they came in with their luggage. And we helped them to carry it. And actually, sometimes had to persuade them not to take too much. Because we are only allowed, if you couldn't carry it, that was it. And-- and what we had to do, load the luggage on the truck or something like that. We did that sometime too. And, of course, what happens with that, some of your classmates are suddenly there. I remember distinctly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course, we had a pretty good idea of what happened. We only knew or talked about, they're going to go to some eastern city. What's it called? It was a [INAUDIBLE] named Litzmanstadt was one. And there they're going to resettle and so on. We doubted it, but well, what could we do? Did you ever receive any mail from people who had been sent away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, on occasion we got a card. And we actually sent some clothes or food. And what you could send it and then get a little card back. Thank you very much. We received it. We are well. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they seem to be mechanical letters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, definitely mechanical letters. Absolutely. Was no doubt about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think the populace knew where people were going? What the destination was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Actually, once the star came, the populace, they probably thought that the population would, oh, rise up and harass the Jews. Maybe attempt in some cases. To me, just the opposite. They came. I remember distinctly one time somebody walked beside me. And suddenly was a can of milk, condensed milk. And people were actually [INAUDIBLE], but supportive. They didn't like it. But It was primarily-- but I heard that in little towns it was different. See, in Berlin if you wanted to help someone, fine, you could do it. Many people. But in a little town, 1,000, 2,000 people, they knew exactly who was where and what would happen. And they didn't go by the book either. That was in the bigger cities, was much bigger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if there was anything Jews could do to improve their chances? Was there some action they could take to forestall or avoid these deportation lists?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I personally don't know how they were put together, the deportation list. Unless you did something. Let's fact it, they were very careful. A traffic violation, they didn't cross the street properly, well, you were-- immediately you were that could wind up on the deportation list. [INAUDIBLE] had to be very careful. And you better be home at 8 o'clock at night. But you really could not do anything. At least I-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I was going to ask if there seemed to be any method to the way the lists were drawn up? Or the way people--

speaker: interviewee
text: The list, it started out to be the lists. Later on there were no lists.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then how did people get selected?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [INAUDIBLE]. And what happened, there was another milestone in 1942 when the school closed. Suddenly came the edict, schools are to be closed. And well, what to teachers? So the teachers, actually, as long as they had a job, they were quite safe from deportation. After that, maybe some were sent to a factory. But many were just deported. And students, they worked either in factories or later on on-- some worked, actually, at the railroad. Some worked at the-- dealing with the luggage, storing and carrying luggage at the railroad station. They had to do some work, forced labor. I personally was-- actually what happened, I started to work at the social service agency. Or some work at--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a Jewish community-run social service?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish community. I  happened to work-- I had to do something. I worked there and I worked-- I kind of run errands, answered the phone, and so on. And it was actually, I should point out, grotesque that the same social services building was a daycare center. And lo and behold, who came to inspect it? There was a regular inspection. All daycare centers had to be inspected. This is a Jewish one. Didn't matter. They came anyway. And what is bizarre is the children, they inspected the children, probably they said, well, they don't have sufficient hand-washing facilities. The same one 10 days later, they deported, see. This was madness. But because it was written, they had to be inspected. Therefore, we go. And that happened. It was right there. Suddenly, oh, so-and-so was coming to inspect the daycare center.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other social services did the gemeinde provide?

speaker: interviewee
text: Primarily, actually, they had a kitchen where people could eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a soup kitchen?

speaker: interviewee
text: A kind of soup kitchen for, I suppose, a nominal charge. Soup kitchen and then people give it-- my father actually worked there for some time. He gave really good advice for free. What to do with property and very often-- and my father also was a guardian of some, I guess, several young people. [INAUDIBLE] a diet and, I would say, at that time to provide it to you, social services.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have health care? Was there still a hospital in operation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was still a hospital. But those didn't-- actually, they did not provide health care. They had to go to a physician or go to the Jewish hospital. It was still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there are still an old-age home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still an old-age home, yes. Some was actually still an old-age home. And others were no longer used as old-age home. They were used as a-- a transit camp. And I got into one of those.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mentioned that my mother helped people carrying the luggage. Well, at one time they wanted to know who are you? Ah ha. Oh, you are married to a Jew. Where are they now? Oh, my husband is there. My-- well, tomorrow they're going to report to a transit camp in the Grosse Hamburger Strasse, the notorious transit camp. It was one of the camps after they collect people, they put-- they collected them. And they collected them in this camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And lo and behold, we had to report. And I remember distinctly it was at the time of my 16th birthday. And at that time were the notorious Brunner, Alois Brunner from Vienna. And he really-- I guess he was called in to show the German Gestapo how it's being done.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he kept us there for several days. And I remember that too we found decent people. There were some regular police guards. And we were not permitted to sit down or lie down during the day. Had to stand up all the time. Who knows why?

speaker: interviewee
text: And occasionally, Brunner got up and to make sure that nobody would sit down. Try to catch them. And before he left his office, that uniformed policeman came up. Get up. Get up. He's coming. He is coming. Don't sit down.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm asking you, why did the man risk his neck doing this? We found decent people too. What-- so he kept us there and-- I remember was interview. Brunner was sitting down there. You walk in the room. There in front, at the head table, the Austrians, Brunner in the middle. He presided. And at the side, along the wall, the other Germans were sitting, learning how it's being done.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they try to give me some trick questions. But I answered, apparently, correctly. And they let me go. Then afterwards, they interviewed my father. And they let us go. Now remember, before going in, I was admonished by my father and others, now answer the question. Don't be a hero. No smart-alecky answers. [INAUDIBLE]. You just answer the question. That's all. And so I did. And they let me go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father gave you good counsel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to have to pause now to change tapes. But I want to come back right to that point.
